11:1: From this chapter onward, the Rambam addresses the precise astronomical calculations needed to determine whether the new moon is visible in the sky. These calculations were used by the court during the era when months were sanctified based on direct observation of the moon.
11:2: There are many opinions among Jewish sages and gentile scholars on matters of astronomy. The Rambam chooses to present the method he considers most correct and well-founded, without entering into debates.
11:3: Over time, better calculation methods were discovered thanks to more precise observations and improved mathematical techniques. The Rambam uses the most up-to-date methods known in his era.
11:4: Although in our time we no longer sanctify months based on observation, it is still important to study these calculations. They are part of the Torah, and anyone capable of understanding them is obligated to engage with them.
11:5: If a scholar finds small discrepancies between the calculations and reality, this is expected and normal. The calculations are based on mathematical approximations, and over time small errors accumulate. The Rambam emphasizes this is not a mistake but rather the nature of any mathematical model.
11:6: A fundamental principle: every circle is divided into 360 degrees. Each degree is divided into 60 parts (minutes), and each minute into 60 seconds, and so on. This is the system of angular measurement used for all the calculations.
11:7: The zodiac is divided into 12 constellations, each spanning 30 degrees (360 total). The constellations in order are: Aries, Taurus, Gemini, Cancer, Leo, Virgo, Libra, Scorpio, Sagittarius, Capricorn, Aquarius, Pisces.
11:8: To convert degrees to a position within a constellation: divide the total degrees by 30. The quotient indicates how many complete constellations have passed, and the remainder is the degree within the current constellation.
11:9: Example: if the sun's position is 200 degrees, divide 200 / 30 = 6 complete constellations and 20 degrees. This means the sun is at 20 degrees of the seventh constellation (Libra).
11:10: Rules for arithmetic with degrees and minutes: when adding two values, add degrees to degrees and minutes to minutes. If the minutes exceed 60, carry over one degree. If the degrees exceed 360, start over (since a full revolution is complete).
11:11: When subtracting degrees and minutes: if the minutes being subtracted are larger, borrow one degree (= 60 minutes) and add it to the minutes. If the degrees being subtracted are larger, add 360 degrees (a full revolution).
11:12: Another rule: when multiplying degrees by a number, multiply the degrees and minutes separately, then combine the results. When dividing, divide the degrees, convert the remainder to minutes, and add them to the existing minutes.
11:13: The sun and moon do not travel in a simple orbit. Each moves on a small circle (epicycle) whose center travels along a larger circle. Because of this, the motion as observed from earth is not uniform -- sometimes faster and sometimes slower.
11:14: The 'mean motion' is the average speed, as if the body moved at a constant rate. The 'true position' is the actual position after correcting for the difference caused by the non-uniform motion.
11:15: The difference between the mean motion and the true position is called the 'equation of the course' (correction). Sometimes the true position is ahead of the mean and sometimes it lags behind, depending on where the body is on the epicycle.
11:16: All calculations in the following chapters are performed for Jerusalem, located approximately at 32 degrees north latitude and 35 degrees east longitude. Jerusalem is considered the center of the world for the purposes of these calculations.
11:17: To calculate for a different location, one must account for the difference in longitude (east-west) from Jerusalem. Each degree of longitude equals approximately 4 minutes of time. Locations east of Jerusalem see the moon earlier, and those to the west see it later.
12:1: The mean motion of the sun is 59 minutes and 8 seconds per day (nearly one full degree). The Rambam provides pre-computed tables: motion for 10 days, 100 days, 1000 days, and a full year (regular and leap) -- to save repetitive calculations.
12:2: The sun has a point of 'height' (apogee) -- the highest point in its orbit. This point moves very slowly. At the epoch ('root') used by the Rambam, the sun's apogee was at 86 degrees and 45 minutes (i.e., near the end of Gemini).
13:1: To find the sun's true position: first compute the mean motion (as described in chapter 12), then apply a correction. The correction depends on the distance between the sun and its apogee.
13:2: The 'equation of the course' (anomaly) is calculated as follows: mean motion minus the sun's apogee. If the result is negative, add 360 degrees. This is the angle that determines how much correction to add or subtract.
13:3: If the anomaly is exactly 180 degrees or 360 degrees (= 0), no correction is needed at all. The mean motion equals the true position, because the sun is at the point where the two circles coincide.
13:4: The Rambam provides a correction table: for every 10 degrees of anomaly (from 10 to 180) there is a corresponding correction value. For example, at 10 degrees the correction is 20 minutes; at 90 degrees the correction reaches its maximum of about 2 degrees and 5 minutes.
13:5: If the anomaly is between 0 and 180 degrees, subtract the correction from the mean motion. That is, the sun's true position is 'behind' the mean.
13:6: If the anomaly is between 180 and 360 degrees, add the correction to the mean motion. In this case, the sun's true position is 'ahead of' the mean.
13:7: When the anomaly is greater than 180, to find the appropriate correction: subtract the anomaly from 360. The result gives the value to look up in the correction table.
13:8: If the anomaly falls between two values in the table (for example 54 degrees, between 50 and 60), use interpolation: compute the difference between the two correction values and take a proportional share based on the distance from the lower value.
13:9: Worked example: if the mean motion is 60 degrees and the sun's apogee is 86 degrees, the anomaly is 360 - (86 - 60) = 334. Since 334 is greater than 180, subtract from 360 to get 26 degrees, look up the correction in the table, and add it.
13:10: To find the true equinox (not just the mean equinox): calculate when the sun actually reaches 0 degrees (head of Aries) or 180 degrees (head of Libra). This can be several days before or after the mean equinox.
13:11: The difference between the true equinox and the mean equinox can reach nearly two days. Therefore, for precise calculations of lunar visibility, it is important to use the true equinox rather than the mean equinox of Shmuel or Rav Ada.
14:1: The moon has two orbits: a large orbit in which it revolves around the earth, and a small orbit (epicycle) in which it moves within the large orbit. The moon's mean motion per day is 13 degrees, 10 minutes, and 35 seconds. This rate is the basis for all lunar position calculations.
14:2: To calculate the moon's position, pre-computed remainders are used: how far the moon moves in 10 days, 100 days, 29 days, a full year, etc. This allows computing the moon's mean position for any desired date by adding the appropriate remainders.
14:3: The moon's 'corrected mean' (anomaly) -- that is, its position on the epicycle -- moves 13 degrees, 3 minutes, and 54 seconds per day. The starting point for calculation ('root') is a known position of the moon at the beginning of Thursday night, 3 Nisan 4938.
14:4: The anomaly remainder for a regular year, a leap year, and a single month are all fixed values that can be added to reach any date. The principle is identical to computing the molad: take a known root and add remainders.
14:5: A seasonal correction must be applied based on the sun's position in the zodiac. When the sun is in certain parts of the zodiac, the moon appears slightly different from what the mean calculation shows, so the mean position is adjusted accordingly.
14:6: The corrected mean position is the moon's position at the moment of observation -- approximately 20 minutes after sunset. This is the time when the new moon's visibility is checked, so all calculations are aimed at this moment.
15:1: To find the moon's true position (not the mean), the 'double elongation' is used -- twice the distance between the sun and the moon. The double elongation is the primary tool for correcting the difference between the mean and true positions.
15:2: It is important to understand: these calculations are intended only for determining the visibility of the new moon, not for general astronomical purposes. The Rambam emphasizes that the method is tailored to a specific halakhic goal -- knowing whether the moon can be seen.
15:3: Take the double elongation and look up in the table how many degrees to add or subtract from the mean position. The table provides different corrections depending on the size of the double elongation, from one degree up to 180 degrees.
15:4: After applying the double elongation correction, a further correction is made based on the moon's anomaly. Take the anomaly value, find the corresponding correction in the table, and add or subtract it -- this yields the moon's true position.
